Memo to Helen
Subject: Your nine-day absence — three triggers, three actions, one mistake owned, one open question for your return

Hi Helen,

Short version: vendor slip handled at routine level, M. Whitfield's question routed and closed Wednesday, finance-figure mistake on Friday's status corrected Monday morning. One open item I'd like your judgment on at the bottom.

Detail.

1. Vendor slip — integration plan.

T. Reyes emailed 5:47 Friday with no new date. The chapter's framing was useful here: this is the project needing me to keep moving, not waiting for you. I emailed her Monday 8:30 with a specific ask: firm date by EOD Monday plus one-line root cause. Tuesday morning she returned with next Wednesday, "internal capacity". I judged this routine vendor-management; the slip is one week, the project plan absorbs it, and pushing for root cause was within my reach. I did not escalate to the covering PM because the situation didn't warrant her time. My escalation criterion if T. Reyes had stayed silent: 24-hour silence triggers covering-PM loop-in for visibility, even if no action needed.

I told Raj and Priya same morning so they could replan their week. Raj switched to the data-validation harness work he'd flagged last week as a useful adjacency; Priya spent two days on documentation prep we had queued for week six. Net week-loss: zero. Net schedule risk: zero, with one week of buffer eaten.

Mindset shift applied: ownership over task. The task list said "wait for vendor". The project needed someone to chase the vendor on day one.

2. M. Whitfield email — split-jurisdiction regulatory question.

I responded Monday 9:30 with what I'd quote as the model from Chapter 2: "Thanks for raising this — I don't have the regulatory detail to answer properly. Routing to R. Silva (tech lead) this morning; I'll come back to you by Wednesday EOD." I did not respond to the tone of his email; I responded to the question. He had been brief and slightly sharp; I read that as brief, not hostile, and matched the brevity.

Routed to R. Silva same morning with the email forwarded and one paragraph of context (urgency, why this stakeholder, what he likely needs). R. Silva came back Tuesday with a written analysis. I sent it to M. Whitfield Wednesday 9 am with you copied. He replied "thanks, that's clear." Loop closed.

Mindset shift applied: refusal to pretend, and composure under pressure. The pull was to either fabricate something to look competent, or apologize my way through a non-answer. Neither was the move.

3. Wrong finance figure on Friday's status.

This was my error. I noticed Friday afternoon — too late to fix the report, which had gone out under your name to the sponsor and four senior stakeholders. The figure was off by £47k against £2.3m budget; not catastrophic, but wrong.

Owned it Monday 9:00 with a single email to the full distribution: "Status correction — finance figure on page 2 was outdated by two weeks. Revised figure £x; variance against budget unchanged at amber. Apologies for the error." Attached the corrected page. No drama, no repeat apologies.

Lesson noted for self: the finance figure on the status report should be cross-checked against the live tracker on Friday morning, not Friday afternoon. I've added a 9 am Friday check to my recurring calendar. Won't happen again.

Mindset shift applied: own / fix / extract. Hiding it until your return was the wrong move and would have made it worse — by then M. Whitfield's office would have been planning against the wrong number.

Open item for your return.

The covering PM. I did not loop her in on any of this because none of it warranted her time. In hindsight I think a one-line "for your awareness" email on Monday afternoon would have been the right move — not to ask her to act, but to keep the covering relationship warm. I'd like your steer on whether the bar for looping in a covering PM is "needs action" or "needs awareness". I lean toward awareness; want your view before the next time this situation arises.

Welcome back. Will write up the longer narrative for Friday's status.

— Coordinator
